text: The surgeon uses intraoperative ultrasound to confirm the location of the tumor and assess its relationship to the underlying vasculature, providing a detailed view of the anatomical structures involved.
The surgeon divides the round and coronary ligaments, detaching them from the anterior abdominal wall. The division of the coronary ligament exposes the underlying hepatic veins.

The surgeon carefully manipulates the liver tissue to avoid injury to the underlying inferior vena cava and phrenic vein branches, minimizing the risk of significant bleeding during the mobilization process.

The surgeon frees the gallbladder from surrounding attachments, ligates and divides the cystic duct and cystic artery, and separates the gallbladder from the hepatocystic plate before removing it from the abdomen.
The surgeon begins hilar dissection by identifying and isolating the portal vein, followed by the right hepatic artery. The right hepatic artery is controlled with clips and then divided.
The surgeon isolates the right portal vein, carefully identifying and protecting the left portal vein before proceeding with the transaction.
The surgeon marks the vascular demarcation line on the liver surface using ledger cautery, indicating the line for parenchymal transection.
The surgeon passes an umbilical tape transabdominally through the foramen of Winslow, preparing for a potential Pringle maneuver.

The surgeon initiates parenchymal transection using a harmonic scalpel for superficial tissue layers. For deeper tissue, a combination of the CUSA device, clips, and bipolar cautery is employed to carefully separate and control the liver parenchyma.

During the parenchymal transection, the surgeon carefully identifies and isolates branches of the middle hepatic vein, individually controlling each branch to ensure precise management of vascular structures.

Upon encountering bleeding, the surgeon uses suction and both hands to expose the vascular injury. The injury is temporarily controlled with the left hand, and more permanent control is achieved using clips or sutures.
The surgeon applies a hemostatic adjunct, such as a Surgicel, to the surgical field to aid in controlling bleeding during the hepatectomy procedure.
The surgeon proceeds with subhepatic dissection after completing a significant amount of parenchymal transection, carefully liberating the liver from its retroperitoneal attachments.
The surgeon performs caudate transection, identifying the inferior right hepatic vein. The vein is then ligated and divided, completing this step of the hepatectomy procedure.
The surgeon divides the already controlled right portal vein and proceeds with hyaluronate plate dissection to identify and control the bile duct.

The surgeon continues deeper parenchymal transection using a combination of the CUSA device and ultrasonic scalpel, carefully identifying and controlling larger hepatic vein branches as they are encountered.
Following the completion of hepatic parenchymal transection, the surgeon isolates the right hepatic vein, controls it, and divides it using a stapling device.

Following the completion of hepatic parenchymal transection and securing inflow and outflow control, the surgeon liberates the liver from its remaining attachments, visibly separating it from surrounding structures.
The surgeon removes the liver specimen from the surgical field, preparing it for pathologic evaluation.
